summary: Gruber responds to Brent Simmons's criticism of Apple's external purchase link policy by exploring the growing fear among Mac developers that Apple might extend iOS-style commission demands to all Mac software. He argues that Apple views iOS and Mac as fundamentally different platforms, and that the Mac's openness to software outside Apple's control is essential to its identity as a PC. While convinced Apple has no intention of imposing commissions on Mac software, he acknowledges that developers who actually sell Mac software don't share his confidence. He warns that merely planting the seed of doubt about such a policy could damage the Mac ecosystem, which already faces a shortage of new native software.
key_points: Multiple indie Mac developers are genuinely worried Apple could demand a 27% cut from all Mac software, not just App Store apps
Apple technically could enforce such a commission since all commercial Mac software must be signed through Apple's developer program
The Mac's essential role is as an open platform for whatever developers can imagine, with no limits beyond available APIs
The move from Intel to Apple silicon proves Apple's long-term commitment to the Mac as a forever platform
Developer uncertainty about selling Mac software could damage the platform even without Apple actually changing policy
money_quotes: Apple's policies for Distributing apps in the U.S. that provide an external purchase link are startlingly graceless and a jarring, but not surprising, reminder that Apple is not a real person and not worthy of your love.
I can't emphasize enough how flabbergasted many developers are — nor how offended.
It's the heaviness of the Mac that allows iOS to remain light.
The Mac today, at 40 years old, is if anything stronger and more essential than it was at 25.
One thing that can't change without destroying it is its openness to software outside Apple's control.
Apple doesn't have to make a platform-destructive money-grab policy change to ruin the Mac. They can ruin it simply by planting the seed of doubt that they might.
This week's External Purchase Link Entitlement policy for iOS is a gust of foul-smelling wind so strong it demands a weather advisory.
Executives at Apple might be greedy, but they're not stupid.
key_insight: Apple's aggressive iOS commission policies are causing real collateral damage to Mac developer confidence, and the mere possibility of extending those policies to the Mac could undermine a platform that depends on developer trust and openness to thrive.
